hook: Haaretz and Anadolu Agency English — opposite political universes — both said the same thing about Israeli buffer zone in southern Lebanon. Nobody else did.

tweets.text: 12 buckets, 20 articles, 7 distinct frames in today's coverage.

Frame: CEASEFIRE_FICTION (4 buckets). "Israeli strikes devastate Lebanese cities despite ceasefire (VIDEOS)

Israeli strikes on Lebanon have destroyed several buildings in Beirut…" — RT

Frame: DRONE_TECHNOLOGY_GAP (2 buckets). "Israel's defense establishment saw solutions for Hezbollah drones – but the IDF has yet to get them

A surge in deadly Hezbollah drone atta…" — Haaretz

Frame: CIVILIAN_DISPLACEMENT_FRAME (2 buckets). "Residents say Israeli strikes targetted families seeking refuge in Lebanon

Residents say Israeli strikes targetted families seeking refuge…" — Middle East Eye

Frame: LITANI_BUFFER_EXPANSION (1 bucket). "Israel attacks villages north of Litani to ease pressure on troops occupying crucial axis

Israel has stepped up strikes on villages north…" — Mada Masr

The paradox: Haaretz (critical Israeli press, typically cautious about amplifying IDF capability gaps) and Anadolu Agency (Turkish state news, routinely adversarial to Israeli military operations) independently arrive at the same assessment: the IDF cannot jam Hezbollah's fiber-…

Silence as data: `russia` didn't cover this — they covered RT covers the strikes and ceasefire violation frame but, unusually, sources the IDF's own statement (40 targets struck, 10 militants killed over the weekend) without adversarial framing—closer to TASS wire output than ed…

**Mada Masr** (egypt): Frames Israeli escalation north of the Litani as pressure-relief for troops on a 'crucial axis'—a specific operational geography angle that reframes the strikes as planned buffer expansion, not retaliation.
